js_004: Who is the American writer and thinker who wrote Walden, the record of a life in the woods?
js_009: Walden was written by Henry David Thoreau (1817–1862), a writer and thinker at the heart of American Transcendentalism.
js_011: By which pond did Thoreau build a cabin and live alone?
js_016: Thoreau lived at Walden Pond. The book takes its title straight from the name of this pond.
js_018: Roughly how long did Thoreau actually stay in the Walden woods?
js_023: Thoreau lived at Walden for 2 years, 2 months, and 2 days — from July 4, 1845 to September 6, 1847.
js_025: Near which American town does Walden Pond lie?
js_030: Walden Pond is near Concord, Massachusetts — also the hometown where Thoreau was born and raised.
js_032: Who owned the land where Thoreau built his cabin and was also his mentor?
js_037: The land belonged to Ralph Waldo Emerson — a leading transcendentalist thinker who was Thoreau’s mentor and patron.
js_039: On what symbolic day did Thoreau begin his life at Walden?
js_044: Thoreau entered the woods on July 4, 1845 — Independence Day, a symbolic date, as if declaring an independence of his own.
js_046: In what year was Walden, Thoreau’s account of the woods, published as a book?
js_051: Walden was published in 1854. After leaving the woods, Thoreau revised the manuscript many times before sending it into the world.
js_053: What did Thoreau build with his own hands to live in at the woods?
js_057: a boat floating on the water
js_058: Thoreau built a log cabin with his own hands, noting every cost down to the nail to show how cheaply a home could be made.
js_060: Which crop did Thoreau grow by hand by the pond to eat and even sell?
js_065: Thoreau grew beans. Walden even has a whole chapter called “The Bean-Field” — that is how symbolic this labor is.
js_067: Which 19th-century American movement of thought did Thoreau belong to, alongside Emerson?
js_072: Thoreau belonged to Transcendentalism — a distinctly American movement that prized nature and individual intuition.
js_079: The Walden Woods Project · Thoreau & Walden
js_080: Henry David Thoreau (1817–1862) was an American writer and thinker born in Concord, Massachusetts — a symbol of the Transcendentalism that sang of nature and self-reliance.
js_081: Walden Pond is a small pond near Concord, Thoreau’s hometown. In the woods by this pond he ran an experiment in living at a distance from civilization.
js_082: To be exact, from July 4, 1845 to September 6, 1847 — 2 years, 2 months, 2 days. The book compresses that time into the four seasons of a single year.
js_083: Walden Pond is in Concord, Massachusetts. Today it is a state reservation and a place of pilgrimage honoring the site of Thoreau’s cabin.
js_084: The land belonged to Thoreau’s mentor, Ralph Waldo Emerson — a leading transcendentalist who gave Thoreau great influence and support.
js_085: Watch out for the mix-up — he entered the woods on July 4, 1845 (Independence Day), while the book came out much later, in 1854. Don’t blur the two years together.
js_086: Walden was published in 1854. Even after leaving the woods, Thoreau kept revising the manuscript to raise its polish.
js_087: Thoreau built the log cabin with his own hands. He noted every cost down to the nail, showing just how little money a home can take.
js_088: Thoreau grew beans to eat and to sell. In “The Bean-Field” he says he was determined to know beans, turning over the meaning of labor and self-reliance.
js_089: Transcendentalism was a 19th-century American movement that prized nature and individual intuition. Emerson led it, and Thoreau lived it out.
js_102: ↻ This one will come back later
js_104: From 1845 to 1847, Thoreau built a cabin by Walden Pond with his own hands and lived at a distance from civilization — a 2-year, 2-month, 2-day experiment in living.
js_106: The fewer your needs, the freer your life becomes. Simplify — the principle Thoreau repeated again and again. Simplicity is not the goal but the way to the essential.
js_108: The mass of people lead lives of quiet desperation. It is Thoreau’s famous insight into those crushed by habit and possessions, who have lost the true life.
js_110: Thoreau grew beans to eat and to sell. Saying he was determined to know beans, he turns over the meaning of self-reliance and being in the plain labor of working the soil by hand.
js_111: The pond rumored to be bottomless, which he measured from the ice to be about 30 meters deep. A symbol of clear, deep self-reflection that breaks superstition with observation.
js_113: Jailed for a night for refusing the poll tax to an unjust state. It later became “Civil Disobedience,” influencing the nonviolent resistance of Gandhi and King.
js_114: A 19th-century American philosophy that prized nature and individual intuition. Led by Emerson and lived out by Thoreau — a philosophy of self-reliance.
js_116: As ice melts in spring, the spirit revives too. Thoreau urges a life of following your own North Star, awake in every moment and focused on the essential.
js_118: Walden as an experiment in living
js_119: The record of Thoreau’s life by Walden Pond, where he built a cabin from 1845 to 1847.
js_120: It compresses the real 2 years, 2 months, 2 days into a single year, spring to spring.
js_121: Beneath it runs Transcendentalism, shared with Emerson — the thought of nature and self-reliance.
js_122: In all 18 chapters, labor, nature, and reflection unfold with the seasons.
js_124: Why he went to the woods (Economy)
js_125: The long first chapter. It reckons, from the accounts up, what a person truly needs.
js_126: Food, shelter, clothing, fuel. Thoreau boils the necessities down to just these.
js_127: This is where the insight comes from: the mass of people lead lives of quiet desperation.
js_128: He records the cabin’s building cost down to the nail, proving a life of little expense.
js_130: Where he lived · reading · sounds · solitude
js_131: The famous motive is revealed: he came to the woods to front the essential facts of life.
js_132: In “Reading,” he counts reading the great classics in the original as the finest training.
js_133: In “Sounds” and “Solitude,” he sings of nature’s sounds and the fullness of being alone.
js_134: Not loneliness, but solitude as a deep calm shared with nature.
js_137: “Visitors” — woodcutters, farmers, friends, and others come to the cabin.
js_138: “The Bean-Field” — growing beans by hand, he turns over the meaning of labor and self-reliance.
js_139: In “The Village,” the night he was jailed for refusing the poll tax flickers by.
js_140: In “The Ponds,” he paints the clear, deep Walden Pond as a mirror for self-reflection.
js_142: House-warming · winter animals · the frozen pond
js_143: “House-Warming” — the heating and housekeeping needed to get through winter.
js_144: “Winter Animals” — he observes the beasts and birds on the snow in fine detail.
js_145: “The Pond in Winter” — from the ice he measures the pond’s depth himself, breaking superstition.
js_146: The deepest point of the pond said to be bottomless was about 30 meters.
js_148: Renewal, and a path of one’s own
js_149: “Spring” — the scene of melting ice symbolizes the renewal and revival of the spirit.
js_150: “Conclusion” — follow your own North Star, he urges, and walk a path of your own.
js_151: The famous line: if you hear a different drummer, step to the beat you hear.
js_152: Beyond simplicity, the book closes on a life awake in every moment.
js_154: Thoreau planted and hoed beans by the pond with his own hands. Saying he was determined to know beans, he draws the meaning of self-reliance and being from that plain labor.
js_155: It makes you think about how labor, not possession, binds a life to the essential.
js_157: Walden Pond was rumored to be bottomless; from the winter ice Thoreau measured it himself and found the deepest point to be about 30 meters.
js_158: It shows Thoreau’s characteristic stance: breaking drifting superstition with his own observation and measurement.
js_160: Opposing slavery and war, Thoreau refused the poll tax paid to the state and was jailed for a night. This experience later became “Civil Disobedience.”
js_161: It makes you ask how an individual conscience can resist unjust power.
js_162: What do I truly need, and what could I do without?
js_163: Picture Thoreau’s food, shelter, clothing, and fuel. Count up what you could take away from your own life.
js_164: Am I perhaps living out of habit, in quiet desperation?
js_165: Look back on today: was it a rhythm someone else set, or a life you meant to live?
js_166: What is real wealth — the size of what you own, or the size of your free time?
js_167: Weigh it against Thoreau’s reckoning: work a little, and spend the rest of your time as yourself.
js_169: You have seen straight through the world of Walden
js_171: You have caught almost all of the core
